When working with large-scale Apache Spark ETL pipelines, a seemingly minor configuration tweak can yield massive performance gains — or cause frustrating bottlenecks.
One such configuration that frequently surfaces in production environments is:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Many data engineers copy-paste this property into their Spark sessions simply because it exists in an upstream boilerplate template. However, if you are architecting data platforms, optimizing slow jobs, or preparing for a senior data engineering interview, you need to understand exactly what this property does, why it exists, and when to flip the switch.
In this deep dive, we will explore this crucial Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) property from first principles with practical, real-world examples.
Understanding the Problem: Why Do We Have Shuffle Partitions?
Whenever Spark performs wide transformations that require data redistribution across executors, it triggers a shuffle. Common culprits include:

• JOIN operations

• GROUP BY and DISTINCT

• ORDER BY

• REPARTITION

• Window Functions
Imagine a Spark job shuffling 100 GB of data. By default, Spark’s fallback shuffle partition count is defined by:
Mathematically, Spark attempts to break down the data into:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In a perfect world, each partition would be exactly 500 MB. Unfortunately, real-world data is rarely distributed evenly due to data skew. Instead, your post-shuffle partition sizes might look like this:

• Partition 1: 4 MB

• Partition 2: 7 MB

• Partition 3: 520 MB

• Partition 4: 2 MB

• Partition 5: 450 MB

• Partition 6: 5 MB
Some partitions are massive, while others contain practically nothing. This imbalance is where performance begins to degrade.
Why Are Small Partitions Bad?
In Spark, one partition equals one task. Even if a partition contains a measly 5 MB of data, Spark must still execute the entire lifecycle of a task:

• Schedule the task via the Cluster Manager.

• Serialize the task closure.

• Launch the JVM task on an executor.

• Allocate executor memory and CPU resources.

• Read the shuffle files from disk/network.

• Return the task status back to the Driver.
When your job generates thousands of tiny partitions, you end up with thousands of tiny tasks. The actual computation might take milliseconds, but the task scheduling and management overhead starts eating up your entire wall-clock time.
This exact pain point is what Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) was engineered to solve.
What is Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)?
Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is a runtime optimization framework introduced in Spark 3.0. Instead of relying solely on static, compile-time query plans based on stale table statistics, AQE re-optimizes the execution plan during runtime.
Once a shuffle stage finishes, AQE pauses, looks at the actual sizes of the generated shuffle files, and dynamically adjusts the next stage. It primarily performs three optimizations:

• Coalescing small partitions (merging tiny shuffle partitions)

• Optimizing skew joins (splitting massive partitions)

• Switching join strategies (e.g., converting a Sort-Merge Join to a Broadcast Hash Join on the fly)
The Mechanics of Partition Coalescing
Suppose a raw shuffle stage produces an array of tiny partitions: [3MB, 5MB, 2MB, 4MB, 6MB, 5MB]. Instead of spinning up 6 separate tasks, AQE dynamically merges them into fewer, healthier chunks (e.g., a single 25MB partition), drastically reducing task scheduling overhead, disk I/O, and metadata tracking.
But how does Spark know exactly how large a coalesced partition should be?
The Advisory Partition Size
Spark relies on a target metric called the advisory partition size:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In large-scale enterprise production env, data teams often bump this default up to 128MB or 256MB to accommodate higher memory capacities on modern cloud workers.
If your total shuffle output is 512 MB and your advisory size is 128 MB, Spark will try to merge the shuffle blocks to hit exactly 4 partitions (512 / 128).
However, this math changes completely based on parallelismFirst.
Enter parallelismFirst
The configurationspark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.parallelismFirst (which defaults to true) serves as a tie-breaker rule for AQE. It forces Spark to choose between two conflicting goals:

• Maximize Parallelism: Keep all available CPU cores as busy as possible.

• Respect Target Size: Ensure partition sizes match advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes.
Scenario A: parallelismFirst=true (The Default)
When set to true, Spark prioritizes keeping task concurrency high, even if it means ignoring your advisory partition size. Spark essentially says: "I would rather give you smaller partitions so that every CPU core in the cluster has something to do right now."
The Math & Behavior:

• Total Shuffle Output: 512 MB

• Advisory Partition Size: 128 MB

• Expected Partitions: 4

• Actual Output with parallelismFirst=true: Spark might output 20 partitions averaging 25 MB each to saturate the cluster's available slots.

• Advantages: Maximizes cluster CPU utilization, highly concurrent jobs on dedicated clusters.

• Disadvantages: Can trigger thousands of excess tasks, inflating scheduling overhead, JVM startup costs, and network noise.
Scenario B: parallelismFirst=false (The Production Choice)
When you flip this property to false, Spark alters its strategy. It care about saturating every single cluster core at that exact microsecond. Instead, it strictly respects your spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes.
The Math & Behavior:

• Total Shuffle Output: 512 MB

• Advisory Partition Size: 128 MB

• Actual Output with parallelismFirst=false: Spark creates exactly 4 partitions of roughly 128 MB each.

• Advantages: Significantly lowers driver scheduling stress, generates larger sequential disk reads, and drastically shrinks shuffle metadata.

• Disadvantages: If the dataset is too small or your advisory size is set too high, some CPU cores might sit idle during the stage.
Why Production Team Set parallelismFirst=false
Consider a shared multi-tenant Databricks or EMR cluster executing dozens of ETL pipelines simultaneously.
If every concurrent job uses the default parallelismFirst=true, a massive cluster-wide bottleneck occurs:

• Job A requests 40,000 tasks (averaging 15 MB/task)

• Job B requests 35,000 tasks

• Job C requests 50,000 tasks
The Spark Driver’s task scheduler becomes heavily throttled trying to coordinate hundreds of thousands of micro-tasks. You will notice symptoms like high Spark driver CPU utilization, lengthy task launch delays, massive garbage collection (GC) pauses, and degraded cluster efficiency.
By implementing the following configuration block:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Key Takeaways

• spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.parallelismFirst determines whether Spark prioritizes maximum concurrent tasks (true) or target partition block sizes (false).

• Keeping the default (true) can inadvertently hurt performance on massive datasets by flooding the cluster manager with tiny tasks.

• Setting it to false alongside an advisory partition size of 128MB or 256MB is a battle-tested pattern for stabilizing enterprise data pipelines.
Data engineering optimization is rarely about finding a “silver bullet” setting — it’s about understanding the architectural trade-offs. The next time you analyze a slow-running stage in the Spark UI, look at your task count and partition sizes. If you see thousands of tasks processing tiny fractions of data, you know exactly which configuration option to change.
